A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread sleet, snow, and freezing rain occurred on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The highest snow accumulations were in the Perryville area, where 4 inches was measured. The snow was mixed with sleet. Elsewhere, one to three inches of snow and sleet was measured from Cape Girardeau County west across Bollinger County. Further south, the precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Doniphan and Poplar Bluff areas east to New Madrid and Charleston. Nearly one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines in those areas. Because ground temperatures remained above freezing for much of the event, the main impact was on elevated surfaces such as trees. An isolated power outage was reported in southern Stoddard County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed early in the morning from western Arkansas into south central Missouri ahead of a strong cold front. This line of storms intensified as it moved east-northeast across the mid Mississippi Valley later in the morning. This line of storms was the final round of heavy rain, which capped a very wet couple days that resulted in storm totals of 2 to 4 inches. Isolated higher amounts up to near 5 inches were reported. Isolated flash flooding was reported in some hilly areas north and west of Cape Girardeau, and a small river rose above flood stage. Although instability was weak, very strong winds aloft facilitated bowing line segments accompanied by damaging winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage survey conducted by the National Weather Service revealed scattered damage caused by straight-line thunderstorm winds. Estimated peak winds were 75 mph. The damage area paralleled Missouri Highway 142 right through the center of the city from 0.5 mile north of Doniphan to one mile south of Doniphan. The maximum width of the damage area was 1.5 miles. Scattered tree damage occurred across Doniphan, including a large pine tree that was uprooted. A carport was turned and damaged. Several metal flat-bottom fishing boats were overturned at a boat shop. A glass door was shattered at the boat shop. A power pole was snapped near a house that sustained light roof and fence damage.

The path length was 15 miles and the maximum width was 1.5 miles. One open storage barn was destroyed, and other nearby barns sustained roof damage. A former school building received minor roof damage. A few homes sustained shingle and roof damage. Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged freezing drizzle and freezing rain event impacted all of eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri from February 5 until February 7. While the bulk of the precipitation fell overnight February 6, into the morning of February 7 the higher impacts appeared to come on February 5, when freezing drizzle caused roads to become slick. In some areas the roads were completely impassible, and numerous injury vehicle accidents occurred. At least two fatalities were caused by the icy roads on February 5, in Lafayette County when a vehicle skidded off the road due to ice. A semi trailing the vehicular accident then impacted the vehicle, killing two of the four occupants. The MHP police report is linked here: https://www.mshp.dps.missouri.gov/HP68/AccidentDetailsAction?ACC_RPT_NUM=190068843 . Aside from those two accidents, there have been numerous injury accidents, mostly resulting in minor injuries, with a handful of moderate to serious injuries. Highway 7 just north of Pleasant Hill was closed for several hours on Tuesday evening because a MODoT salt truck among other vehicles impacted the guard rail just north of town.||Without any notable break in the freezing precipitation the ice continued to form on trees and power lines through Wednesday February 6, resulting in most locations seeing around 1/8 to 1/4 of ice through Wednesday afternoon. On Wednesday night heavy rain and sleet moved into the area, and added to the already robust ice amounts. With some modest instability in the atmosphere many of these storms produced lightning, a rare occurrence! One storm did produce some quarter-sized hail near Clinton, MO. By the end of the event almost all locations within the Ice Storm Warning (which was issued Wednesday afternoon - 2/6) received around 1/4 to 1/2, with some of the higher totals in Johnson County, MO approaching .60.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While light freezing drizzle occurred off and on February 5, the bulk of the freezing rain fell during the overnight period on February 6 into February 7. Over the course of the event DeKalb County received approximately a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Numerous vehicle accidents occurred area-wide and minor tree damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient across the region generated wind gusts in excess of 50 knots in a number of locations as well as sustained winds of 35 mph or higher, especially during the early morning hours through midday on November 27th.

A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread sleet, snow, and freezing rain occurred on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The highest snow accumulations were in the Perryville area, where 4 inches was measured. The snow was mixed with sleet. Elsewhere, one to three inches of snow and sleet was measured from Cape Girardeau County west across Bollinger County. Further south, the precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Doniphan and Poplar Bluff areas east to New Madrid and Charleston. Nearly one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines in those areas. Because ground temperatures remained above freezing for much of the event, the main impact was on elevated surfaces such as trees. An isolated power outage was reported in southern Stoddard County.

A slow moving frontal boundary was a dividing line between cold air with temperatures below freezing over west central Missouri into southeast Kansas. While convection with damaging winds and large hail developed in the warm air south of the front, freezing rain and sleet was common to the north of the front. Significant icing occurred over parts of central and west central Missouri and into southeast Kansas and portions of southwest Missouri. The heaviest freezing rain and ice accumulations occurred during the late evening of the February 6th into the morning hours of February 7. In addition, severe storms with damaging winds and flooding occurred to the south of the front in the warmer air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to golf ball size was reported 4 miles west of Highway 97 on Highway 60 near Pierce City in a rural area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought 3 to 5 inches of snow to portions of central Missouri, while freezing rain brought ice accumulations of one to two tenths of an inch along the Highway 60 corridor. There were a number of accidents across southern and central Missouri, including a 50 car pile up on Highway 54. The Southwest District of the Missouri Department of Transportation spread 5000 tons of salt at a cost of nearly $500,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of between 0.10 and 0.15 inches of ice accumulation were reported in and around Ava, with numerous accidents and slide-offs being reported. There was 1 indirect fatality reported near Ava.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east-northeast from the lower Mississippi Valley to the Carolinas. On the north side of this low, widespread sleet, snow, and freezing rain occurred on the afternoon and evening of the 15th. The highest snow accumulations were in the Perryville area, where 4 inches was measured. The snow was mixed with sleet. Elsewhere, one to three inches of snow and sleet was measured from Cape Girardeau County west across Bollinger County. Further south, the precipitation was mostly in the form of freezing rain from the Doniphan and Poplar Bluff areas east to New Madrid and Charleston. Nearly one-quarter inch of ice glazed trees and power lines in those areas. Because ground temperatures remained above freezing for much of the event, the main impact was on elevated surfaces such as trees. An isolated power outage was reported in southern Stoddard County.

A slow moving frontal boundary was a dividing line between cold air with temperatures below freezing over west central Missouri into southeast Kansas. While convection with damaging winds and large hail developed in the warm air south of the front, freezing rain and sleet was common to the north of the front. Significant icing occurred over parts of central and west central Missouri and into southeast Kansas and portions of southwest Missouri. The heaviest freezing rain and ice accumulations occurred during the late evening of the February 6th into the morning hours of February 7. In addition, severe storms with damaging winds and flooding occurred to the south of the front in the warmer air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was struck by lightning just north of Mountain Grove.  The home suffered damage to a couple of rooms by fire and smoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged freezing drizzle and freezing rain event impacted all of eastern Kansas and western and central Missouri from February 5 until February 7. While the bulk of the precipitation fell overnight February 6, into the morning of February 7 the higher impacts appeared to come on February 5, when freezing drizzle caused roads to become slick. In some areas the roads were completely impassible, and numerous injury vehicle accidents occurred. At least two fatalities were caused by the icy roads on February 5, in Lafayette County when a vehicle skidded off the road due to ice. A semi trailing the vehicular accident then impacted the vehicle, killing two of the four occupants. The MHP police report is linked here: https://www.mshp.dps.missouri.gov/HP68/AccidentDetailsAction?ACC_RPT_NUM=190068843 . Aside from those two accidents, there have been numerous injury accidents, mostly resulting in minor injuries, with a handful of moderate to serious injuries. Highway 7 just north of Pleasant Hill was closed for several hours on Tuesday evening because a MODoT salt truck among other vehicles impacted the guard rail just north of town.||Without any notable break in the freezing precipitation the ice continued to form on trees and power lines through Wednesday February 6, resulting in most locations seeing around 1/8 to 1/4 of ice through Wednesday afternoon. On Wednesday night heavy rain and sleet moved into the area, and added to the already robust ice amounts. With some modest instability in the atmosphere many of these storms produced lightning, a rare occurrence! One storm did produce some quarter-sized hail near Clinton, MO. By the end of the event almost all locations within the Ice Storm Warning (which was issued Wednesday afternoon - 2/6) received around 1/4 to 1/2, with some of the higher totals in Johnson County, MO approaching .60.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While light freezing drizzle occurred off and on February 5, the bulk of the freezing rain fell during the overnight period on February 6 into February 7. Over the course of the event Livingston County received approximately a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Numerous vehicle accidents occurred area-wide and minor tree damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A levee failed along the Missouri River near Petersburg, Missouri. Areas along the north bank of the river became inundated with several feet of running water as the river spilled out of of the levee banks. The towns of Franklin and New Franklin were heaviest impacted with several inches of water running into the towns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The breakdown of the Howard County District #2 levee caused at least two other levees to fail, which caused more running water to enter into the farmlands of southern Howard County, as well as the towns of Franklin and New Franklin. The overall value of the damage from this event is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of June 15 scattered severe thunderstorms formed in northwest Missouri, and brought strong winds and large hail These storms also produced a weak and brief tornado near Mound City, Missouri. Little to no damage occurred from this tornado aside from some trees and light objects in the Mound City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a report of a tornado was received by the office investigation using online news and inquiries to local business helped identify the path of this brief and weak tornado through portions of Mound City. Little to no damage occurred outside of several tree limbs and light objects.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level storm system tracked across eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois, and northeast Missouri Thursday night and Friday morning. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer near Memphis reported 2.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: MCV moved through region during the afternoon hours on June 15th with some isolated supercells. Then main cold front moved through during the late evening and overnight hours with a line of convection. There was one tornado and several reports of damaging winds and large hail with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A levee failed along the Missouri River near Petersburg, Missouri. Areas along the north bank of the river became inundated with several feet of running water as the river spilled out of of the levee banks. The towns of Franklin and New Franklin were heaviest impacted with several inches of water running into the towns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the flash flooding as a result of a levee failure near Petersburg, Missouri. Howard County District #2 levee failed the night before, allowing the already flooding Missouri River to spill into low lying areas of southern Howard County. Most of this area consisted of farmland, but the cities of Franklin and New Franklin received several inches of running water. News articles from across the local region indicated that crops planted earlier in the growing season in the southern part of the county were lost. Monetary damages are unknown from this flood event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As strong solar heating destabilized the atmosphere, numerous clusters of intense thunderstorms blossomed from southeast Missouri to southwest Indiana. Damaging winds up to around 60 mph occurred with several of the storms. The environment was characterized by moderate to strong instability and moderate wind shear. Effective bulk wind shear values of 25 to 40 knots were observed. The storms developed east of a weak low pressure center over central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near the intersection of U.S. Highway 62 and Missouri Route 77, blocking both roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated winds gusted to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters and short lines of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours. A few of these were accompanied by damaging winds and large hail. A second round of storms occurred during the evening hours. A couple of supercells developed over southeast Missouri and produced large hail up to ping pong ball size, along with damaging winds approaching 70 mph. Both rounds of storms developed south of a surface low that gradually deepened as it tracked east along the Interstate 70 corridor from Missouri into Illinois. Thunderstorm development was enhanced by large-scale ascent pivoting from the Ozark Plateau into the lower Ohio Valley, ahead of a low amplitude mid-level trough. Daytime heating helped to increase instability, with mixed layer capes approaching 2000 J/kg. Vertical wind shear increased as 40 to 50-knot westerly winds gradually propagated eastward from the south central Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and early evening hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms formed over the central Plains states during the night of the 20th. This bowing line of storms progressed southeast across the St. Louis area during the midday hours, then down across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri during the afternoon. Large areas of wind damage were reported in parts of southeast Missouri. The atmosphere ahead of the storms became strongly unstable, with surface-based capes exceeding 4,000. Given the strong buoyancy, wind profiles were sufficiently strong for widespread severe weather. Deep-layer (0-6 km) wind shear values were no higher than about 30 knots, and 0-1 km shear was about 20 knots. Even though the region was under a broad 500 mb ridge, a moderately strong low-level feed of very moist and unstable air fueled the intense storms as they propagated southeastward.

This system brought rain to the western half of the area during the early morning and scattered storms to locations along and south of Interstate 44 during the afternoon and early evening. Most locations received between one half inch and one inch of rain, with locations near Cassville receiving over three inches. This led to flash flooding in the town of Cassville. Isolated severe weather was reported with this storms system across the Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarters in size was reported by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Pin Oak Levee in Winfield, MO was breached during the afternoon hours of June 2nd, flooding eastern portions of Winfield east of Highway 79.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winfield Pin Oaks Levee system was breached during the afternoon hours on Sunday, June 2nd. The breach was at least 100 feet wide. The water rose quickly and reached the houses in the area beginning around 6 pm. An estimated 150 homes were affected by the levee breach and 250 people evacuated the area. No deaths or injuries occurred due to this levee breach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms entered southeast Missouri about mid-afternoon and then tracked east across western Kentucky and far southern Illinois late in the afternoon and early in the evening. Corridors of wind damage were associated with this line. The storms occurred under a rather strong southwest wind flow ahead of a weak cold front over far western Missouri. A 500 mb shortwave trough over the western high Plains provided some additional support for the storms. The air mass was strongly unstable, with surface capes over 3000 j/kg.

Loosely organized clusters of thunderstorms produced isolated damaging winds, flash flooding, and large hail. A mesoscale vorticity center from earlier thunderstorms moved eastward across southern Missouri during the afternoon. Very warm and moist air in advance of this vorticity center contributed to strong instability, with mixed-layer capes around 3000. Around 25 to 40 knots of westerly flow in the mid levels of the atmosphere was sufficient for loosely organized thunderstorm clusters, a few of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down.

Around 25 to 40 knots of westerly flow in the mid levels of the atmosphere was sufficient for loosely organized thunderstorm clusters, a few of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on Interstate 57 at mile marker 20 near the Mississippi River. The tree was cleared by the Missouri Department of Transportation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of an early morning thunderstorm complex, a line of storms formed as a result of daytime heating in the clearing area behind the first complex. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms entered southeast Missouri about mid-afternoon and then tracked east across western Kentucky and far southern Illinois late in the afternoon and early in the evening. Corridors of wind damage were associated with this line. The storms occurred under a rather strong southwest wind flow ahead of a weak cold front over far western Missouri. A 500 mb shortwave trough over the western high Plains provided some additional support for the storms. The air mass was strongly unstable, with surface capes over 3000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated 60 mph winds at exit 93 on Interstate 55, at the interchange with State Route 74.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters and short lines of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours. A few of these were accompanied by damaging winds and large hail. A second round of storms occurred during the evening hours. A couple of supercells developed over southeast Missouri and produced large hail up to ping pong ball size, along with damaging winds approaching 70 mph. Both rounds of storms developed south of a surface low that gradually deepened as it tracked east along the Interstate 70 corridor from Missouri into Illinois. Thunderstorm development was enhanced by large-scale ascent pivoting from the Ozark Plateau into the lower Ohio Valley, ahead of a low amplitude mid-level trough. Daytime heating helped to increase instability, with mixed layer capes approaching 2000 J/kg. Vertical wind shear increased as 40 to 50-knot westerly winds gradually propagated eastward from the south central Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in typical flood-prone areas of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding continued on parts of the Mississippi River through the month of June. The region remained stuck in a long-term wet pattern that continued through the spring and into early summer. Most locations were 1 to 2 inches above normal for the month of June. Some locations saw as much as 3 to 5 inches more rainfall than normal in June. Heavy rain in the upper parts of the Midwest sent another flood wave toward the middle part of the Mississippi, and another crest occurred around the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River experienced major flooding above the confluence with the Ohio River. The river gage upriver at Cape Girardeau crested at 46.36 feet, which was the fifth highest crest on record. However, the river gage downriver at New Madrid only experienced minor flooding. Mississippi County is mostly agricultural, and most of the impacts were on cropland. County roads outside of the levee system were flooded. State Route VV was flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed over the Missouri Ozarks from the afternoon of the 3rd to the evening of the 7th as several upper level disturbances and an upper level trough moved from the southwest U.S. to the lower Mississippi Valley.  The first round of storms developed during the afternoon and evening of the 3rd, and set the stage for isolated flash flooding on the afternoon of the 4th, mainly over central and southwest Missouri. Greater than two inches of rain fell quickly near Fort Leonard Wood, Mountain Grove and Nixa. Isolated severe wind gusts also occurred from the mid to late afternoon of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low water crossing on Highway 142 was flooded and closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and early evening hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms formed over the central Plains states during the night of the 20th. This bowing line of storms progressed southeast across the St. Louis area during the midday hours, then down across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri during the afternoon. Large areas of wind damage were reported in parts of southeast Missouri. The atmosphere ahead of the storms became strongly unstable, with surface-based capes exceeding 4,000. Given the strong buoyancy, wind profiles were sufficiently strong for widespread severe weather. Deep-layer (0-6 km) wind shear values were no higher than about 30 knots, and 0-1 km shear was about 20 knots. Even though the region was under a broad 500 mb ridge, a moderately strong low-level feed of very moist and unstable air fueled the intense storms as they propagated southeastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 72 mph was measured at the Cape Girardeau airport. Tree limbs were down in Scott City.

Although this was the fifth highest flood crest on record at Cape Girardeau, relatively few homes were flooded because of migration out of the flood plain in recent decades. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters and short lines of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours. A few of these were accompanied by damaging winds and large hail. A second round of storms occurred during the evening hours. A couple of supercells developed over southeast Missouri and produced large hail up to ping pong ball size, along with damaging winds approaching 70 mph. Both rounds of storms developed south of a surface low that gradually deepened as it tracked east along the Interstate 70 corridor from Missouri into Illinois. Thunderstorm development was enhanced by large-scale ascent pivoting from the Ozark Plateau into the lower Ohio Valley, ahead of a low amplitude mid-level trough. Daytime heating helped to increase instability, with mixed layer capes approaching 2000 J/kg. Vertical wind shear increased as 40 to 50-knot westerly winds gradually propagated eastward from the south central Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along U.S. Highway 61 near Haywood City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Poplar Bluff, several trees were blown down, and at least three small trailers were overturned. Just east of Poplar Bluff at and near the airport, damaging winds caused significant damage. A wind gust to 67 mph was measured by the Poplar Bluff airport's automated weather observation system. A parked small plane was overturned and pushed into a building. In the same general area, several telephone poles were knocked down. Numerous tree limbs and power lines were down. This damage was produced by a supercell thunderstorm that tracked east-southeast to New Madrid County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms developed over the Missouri Ozarks from the afternoon of the 3rd to the evening of the 7th as several upper level disturbances and an upper level trough moved from the southwest U.S. to the lower Mississippi Valley.  The first round of storms developed during the afternoon and evening of the 3rd, and set the stage for isolated flash flooding on the afternoon of the 4th, mainly over central and southwest Missouri. Greater than two inches of rain fell quickly near Fort Leonard Wood, Mountain Grove and Nixa. Isolated severe wind gusts also occurred from the mid to late afternoon of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60 mph winds were reported that downed limbs 5 miles east of Lamar on Highway 160.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall caused rapid rises to occur on the Beaver Branch and Indian Creek. A home along Beaver Branch was swept off its foundation and washed downstream. An elderly woman in the home was unable to escape and perished in the flood waters. A bridge on Main Street received significant damage from rapidly moving high water where Beaver Branch flows through the middle of town. Significant damage occurred to a building in the vicinity of the bridge. Numerous evacuations were performed by emergency crews from homes, apartments and businesses. Around 80 homes and businesses received water damage as water several inches deep entered the structures. Some structures had as much as 3 feet of water in them.  The dollar damage amount is a summary for all damages in McDonald County for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe weather outbreak occurred during the afternoon and early evening hours. An organized line of severe thunderstorms formed over the central Plains states during the night of the 20th. This bowing line of storms progressed southeast across the St. Louis area during the midday hours, then down across the lower Ohio Valley and southeast Missouri during the afternoon. Large areas of wind damage were reported in parts of southeast Missouri. The atmosphere ahead of the storms became strongly unstable, with surface-based capes exceeding 4,000. Given the strong buoyancy, wind profiles were sufficiently strong for widespread severe weather. Deep-layer (0-6 km) wind shear values were no higher than about 30 knots, and 0-1 km shear was about 20 knots. Even though the region was under a broad 500 mb ridge, a moderately strong low-level feed of very moist and unstable air fueled the intense storms as they propagated southeastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Within the Ozark National Scenic Riverways, a tree fell on a boat on the Current River. The boat was traveling downstream in the vicinity of the Waymeyer Shute river access point. The 22-year-old male driver of the boat was killed, and two passengers in their 20's were moderately injured. The injured parties were from the local area and were treated at a local hospital. In the same general vicinity, trees and large tree limbs were down on the north end of M Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy late winter and spring precipitation, mixed with a loaded snow pack caused the Missouri River to flood in March. Consistent heavy rains continued the Missouri River flooding into and through June. Some locations along the Missouri River were in major flood for several weeks as heavy rains continued. While the flooding continued through June, there was some gradual and steady improvement across the Missouri River flood plain by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy spring rains caused ongoing flooding along the Missouri River to Continue through the month. Some locations along the Missouri River experienced major flooding at times during the month. Damage estimates from roads washed out and crop damage are unknown at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms entered southeast Missouri about mid-afternoon and then tracked east across western Kentucky and far southern Illinois late in the afternoon and early in the evening. Corridors of wind damage were associated with this line. The storms occurred under a rather strong southwest wind flow ahead of a weak cold front over far western Missouri. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major Mississippi River flooding continued into June as rivers remained high during the month of May. Heavy rain in the later part of the month resulted in the river rising back above major flood stage in late May and flooding continued into the first 6 days of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Gregory Landing began June above its major flood of 25.0 feet and fell below major flood stage around 5 AM on June.  The river crested at 26.97 feet  on June 1st. This is preliminarily the third highest crest record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Loosely organized clusters of thunderstorms produced isolated damaging winds, flash flooding, and large hail. A mesoscale vorticity center from earlier thunderstorms moved eastward across southern Missouri during the afternoon. Very warm and moist air in advance of this vorticity center contributed to strong instability, with mixed-layer capes around 3000. Around 25 to 40 knots of westerly flow in the mid levels of the atmosphere was sufficient for loosely organized thunderstorm clusters, a few of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of water was over the roadway at the intersection of FF Highway and County Road 327. Flooding was reported in Van Buren.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding continued on parts of the Mississippi River through the month of June. The region remained stuck in a long-term wet pattern that continued through the spring and into early summer. Most locations were 1 to 2 inches above normal for the month of June. Some locations saw as much as 3 to 5 inches more rainfall than normal in June. Heavy rain in the upper parts of the Midwest sent another flood wave toward the middle part of the Mississippi, and another crest occurred around the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak of a very prolonged river flood occurred about mid month. At the Cape Girardeau river gage, the river crested at 46.36 feet on the 10th. This was the 5th highest flood crest on record at Cape Girardeau, just slightly above the May 2011 flood but two feet below the Great Flood of 1993. The river rose above flood stage in mid March and stayed above flood stage into the summer. The crests in April and May were not as high as the June crest. There was extensive flooding of low-lying farmland and woods. About 21,000 total acres were flooded, of which 14,000 acres were cropland. Over a dozen county roads were closed in the county. The Red Star neighborhood of Cape Girardeau was flooded. Flooding of the diversion channel floodplain affected areas as far west as Dutchtown and Allenville. Shelters were opened for flood evacuees. Highway 74 was closed, including the on and off ramps from Interstate 55 at exit 93. Facilities at riverfront parks were closed, including the campground and boat ramp at Trail of Tears State Park. Railroad tracks through the county were shut down. The Army Corps of Engineers activated teams to conduct routine inspections of floodwalls and levees.

Greater than two inches of rain fell quickly near Fort Leonard Wood, Mountain Grove and Nixa. Isolated severe wind gusts also occurred from the mid to late afternoon of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low water crossing at Route K near Route MM was closed due to flooding.

The first round of storms developed during the afternoon and evening of the 3rd, and set the stage for isolated flash flooding on the afternoon of the 4th, mainly over central and southwest Missouri. Greater than two inches of rain fell quickly near Fort Leonard Wood, Mountain Grove and Nixa. Isolated severe wind gusts also occurred from the mid to late afternoon of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported over parts of the roadway on Highway JJ, just south of Highway 125.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two clusters of strong to severe storms that developed to the west and northwest of the Ozarks affected southeast Kansas and west central Missouri. The first occurred during the early morning hours of the 6th and the second round moved pretty much the same area on the late evening of the 6th and early morning of the 7th. These storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls occurred in Newton County, and wind gusts to 80 mph occurred in Barton County near Lamar.  Rainfall accumulations averaged around one and a half to two inches in this region, with isolated accumulations over three inches. This caused to Little Osage, Sac, and Marmaton rivers to rise back above flood stage and flash flooding to occur across portions of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred at Echo Bluff State Park and Sinking Creek flooded out of its banks. The swift moving high water destroyed a concrete driveway at a residence approximately 7 miles north northeast of Round Spring. A few county roadways were significantly damaged, and a large area of fencing was damaged as 4.50 inches of rain was measured at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening and early overnight hours of August 15th, into the morning of the 16th scattered thunderstorms formed across eastern Kansas and western Missouri. These storms produced some strong winds across portions of the KC Metro area, but they became significant when they moved eastward toward the Missouri State Fairgrounds in Sedalia, Missouri. Several upstream observations recorded nearly 80 mph winds as the complex approached the fairgrounds. The Sedalia ASOS recorded 67 mph winds after the storm moved through the city and the fairgrounds. Despite the strong winds little to no damage occurred, and no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many large trees were down in Warrensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River returned above flood stage after only a one-month respite from the prolonged flooding that dominated most of the year. After a brief flash drought in September, October ranked as the third wettest October at Cape Girardeau. Heavy rain in the upper Mississippi Valley sent a minor flood crest downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the Mississippi River inundated low-lying fields and woodlands, as well as some riverfront park land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient across the region generated wind gusts in excess of 50 knots in a number of locations as well as sustained winds of 35 mph or higher, especially during the early morning hours through midday on November 27th.

A vigorous low in the mid levels of the atmosphere raced out of the central Rockies and became even stronger as it moved into the upper Midwest. As a result, the surface pressure gradient increased across the region. A cold front swept rapidly eastward during the early morning hours, causing winds to shift into the west and increase even further. Peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 50 mph at most locations. The highest measured gust was 49 mph at a mesonet site on the Mississippi River near New Madrid. There were unofficial gusts near 50 mph in the Ozark foothills, where some tree damage was reported in Carter County.

Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Springfield/Branson National Airport measured a 24 hour rainfall total of 2.87 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient across the region generated wind gusts in excess of 50 knots in a number of locations as well as sustained winds of 35 mph or higher, especially during the early morning hours through midday on November 27th.

A strong cold front and deep upper level trough affected the region from the early morning of the 10th through the mid-morning hours of the 11th. While some sporadic hail and wind reports were received, this system was much more of a heavy rainfall producer. By the late morning of the 10th, flash flooding was already occurring near the Oklahoma and Kansas borders. A State Highway was closed due to flooding in Ozark County and numerous low water crossings and county roads were flooded in southern and central Missouri. ||The largest amounts of rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 10th, with widespread rainfall amounts of two to three inches east of a Joplin to Osceola line, and isolated amounts over 5 inches near Branson and Eldon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station EW3832 in Branson reported 3.42 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low in the mid levels of the atmosphere raced out of the central Rockies and became even stronger as it moved into the upper Midwest. As a result, the surface pressure gradient increased across the region. A cold front swept rapidly eastward during the early morning hours, causing winds to shift into the west and increase even further. Peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 50 mph at most locations. The highest measured gust was 49 mph at a mesonet site on the Mississippi River near New Madrid. There were unofficial gusts near 50 mph in the Ozark foothills, where some tree damage was reported in Carter County.
A strongly forced line of thunderstorms moved across the Ozarks and produced multiple wind gust in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that 85 mph straight line winds destroyed three barns/outbuildings. Two other barns were damaged. Metal pieces from the buildings were blown across a pasture. Minor roof damage occurred to two homes. A few trees limbs and trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flooded parking lot with a partially submerged car was reported. A picture was received via social media. This was a continuation of flooding that began on April 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Finley River was flooded over Riverdale Road for several hours. This was a continuation of flooding that began on April 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and deep upper level trough affected the region from the early morning of the 10th through the mid-morning hours of the 11th. While some sporadic hail and wind reports were received, this system was much more of a heavy rainfall producer. By the late morning of the 10th, flash flooding was already occurring near the Oklahoma and Kansas borders. A State Highway was closed due to flooding in Ozark County and numerous low water crossings and county roads were flooded in southern and central Missouri. ||The largest amounts of rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 10th, with widespread rainfall amounts of two to three inches east of a Joplin to Osceola line, and isolated amounts over 5 inches near Branson and Eldon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of quarter size hail was received via MPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the Mississippi River gradually abated during the month of July as the weather pattern turned somewhat drier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although the Mississippi River remained above flood stage all month, the flooding was very minor by the end of July. This extended flood began in mid March and peaked in June at the fifth highest crest on record at Cape Girardeau. While the crest was among the five highest on record, the extended duration of the flood played an even greater role on the impact. The saturation and resultant weakening of levees permitted water to seep into normally dry areas. Around 21,000 acres were flooded in the county, including over 10,000 acres of cropland. This resulted in millions of dollars in crop losses. Flooding of the diversion channel floodplain in the southern end of the county affected places as far west as Dutchtown. The water receded from the vast majority of the county by the end of July, however a riverfront park and trail in Cape Girardeau remained under water through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived bow echo quickly became less organized as it moved southeast across parts of southeast Missouri. Before weakening, the line of storms caused strong to isolated damaging winds in places west through north of Cape Girardeau. The line of storms weakened as it moved south of the better mid-level wind fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in downtown Cape Girardeau. The flooding was partially due to the very high water levels on the Mississippi River. Pumps that normally pump rain water across the Mississippi River floodwall into the river were unable to keep up with the heavy rainfall rates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large high in the upper levels of the atmosphere was sprawled across the southern two-thirds of the country for several days. This high brought several days of oppressive heat and humidity across southeast Missouri. An outdoor marketplace in Cape Girardeau closed early due to the heat. Afternoon heat indices peaked between 105 and 115 degrees each day from the 17th to the 21st. The heat index at Poplar Bluff peaked at 115 degrees on the 17th, 113 on the 18th, and 108 to 109 from the 19th to the 21st. At Cape Girardeau, the heat index peaked from 105 to 109 each of those five afternoons. Just prior to this heat wave, the remnants of Tropical Storm Barry produced a widespread soaking rain that set the stage for very high humidity levels. Dew points were sometimes in the upper 70's. Actual high temperatures were in the lower 90's.

A large complex of thunderstorms progressed from southeast Iowa into northeast Missouri on the afternoon of July 17th. These storms produced winds of 60 to 70 MPH in Scotland County, causing damage to trees. Additionally, torrential rains of 1 to 2 inches were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriff's Office reported that several trees were down on roadways in Bible Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the late morning to the early afternoon three kayakers entered Bull Creek after heavy rainfall caused the river to rise nearly 14 feet. When the kayakers entered the river a river gauge on Bull Creek recorded a water level  near 13 feet, or near 9 feet above normal stage. The kayakers entered the river near Round Mountain Road and capsized about a half mile down stream where a low water bridge crossed the river. Two of the three kyakers were pulled under by a siphon/whirlpool and drowned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the Mississippi River gradually abated during the month of July as the weather pattern turned somewhat drier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mississippi River floodwaters gradually receded through the month of July. Thousands of acres were inundated early in the month, including a great deal of crop acreage. A number of county roads were flooded near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large high in the upper levels of the atmosphere was sprawled across the southern two-thirds of the country for several days. This high brought several days of oppressive heat and humidity across southeast Missouri. An outdoor marketplace in Cape Girardeau closed early due to the heat. Afternoon heat indices peaked between 105 and 115 degrees each day from the 17th to the 21st. The heat index at Poplar Bluff peaked at 115 degrees on the 17th, 113 on the 18th, and 108 to 109 from the 19th to the 21st. At Cape Girardeau, the heat index peaked from 105 to 109 each of those five afternoons. Just prior to this heat wave, the remnants of Tropical Storm Barry produced a widespread soaking rain that set the stage for very high humidity levels. Dew points were sometimes in the upper 70's. Actual high temperatures were in the lower 90's.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station NSOM7 at the Neosho water station reported 2.16 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low in the mid levels of the atmosphere raced out of the central Rockies and became even stronger as it moved into the upper Midwest. As a result, the surface pressure gradient increased across the region. A cold front swept rapidly eastward during the early morning hours, causing winds to shift into the west and increase even further. Peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 50 mph at most locations. The highest measured gust was 49 mph at a mesonet site on the Mississippi River near New Madrid. There were unofficial gusts near 50 mph in the Ozark foothills, where some tree damage was reported in Carter County.

Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-op observer reported a 24-hr rainfall accumulation of 2.72 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed as daytime heating destabilized the atmosphere ahead of a cold front. Temperatures in the lower to mid 90's and dew points in the mid 70's resulted in strong instability. The cold front crossed the western half of Illinois and southeast Missouri during the afternoon hours. The slow movement of the storms through tropical humidity resulted in torrential rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large high in the upper levels of the atmosphere was sprawled across the southern two-thirds of the country for several days. This high brought several days of oppressive heat and humidity across southeast Missouri. An outdoor marketplace in Cape Girardeau closed early due to the heat. Afternoon heat indices peaked between 105 and 115 degrees each day from the 17th to the 21st. The heat index at Poplar Bluff peaked at 115 degrees on the 17th, 113 on the 18th, and 108 to 109 from the 19th to the 21st. At Cape Girardeau, the heat index peaked from 105 to 109 each of those five afternoons. Just prior to this heat wave, the remnants of Tropical Storm Barry produced a widespread soaking rain that set the stage for very high humidity levels. Dew points were sometimes in the upper 70's. Actual high temperatures were in the lower 90's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th and into the early morning hours of May 24th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. On the anniversary of the Joplin Tornado off 2011, another tornado, rated EF-3 struck the north side of Joplin. This storm system eventually produced subsequent tornadoes in Golden City, Eldon and Jefferson City. Unfortunately there were three fatalities as a result of the tornado in Golden City.  The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads remained closed across portions of Newton County from high water or flood damage to road surfaces. In some cases, paved roads were completely washed out. Some roads were expected to remain closed for weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several shortwaves moved through the region with several waves of showers and storms through the evening on August 25th and into the early morning hours on August 26th. Up to 6 inches of rain fell, along and just south of I-70 over portions of east central Missouri. There were numerous reports of flash flooding in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding, mainly along and south of Interstate 70 in Montgomery County. Numerous roads were flooded including portions of the south outer road along I-70 near mile marker 186. MODOT closed Route K between Route HH and State Highway 19 due to flash flooding. They also closed Route Y east of State Highway 19 just east of Big Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms from the previous day left an outflow boundary along the Arkansas border that helped produce strong to severe storms during the afternoon of the 21st. A final round of storms moved into southeast Kansas and western Missouri during the late evening of the 21st into the early morning of the 22nd. Reports of hail to the size of quarters, wind damage to trees and power lines were received from Nixa to Rolla with the first round of storms. Isolated reports of flash flooding were received with the second and third round of storms from Howell and Newton Counties, where two to three inches of rain fell in a one to two hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route W in the Peace Valley area was flooded in three locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-op observer reported a 24-hr rainfall accumulation of 4.90 inches.

Widespread wind damage occurred across southwest and south central Missouri, mainly south of Interstate 44. NWS Storm Damage Surveys concluded that eight tornadoes occurred along the squall line, affecting locations in and near the communities of Sparta, Rogersville, Highlandville, Licking and Willow Springs, as well as areas on the southeast side of Springfield, and along Wilson���s Creek south of Battlefield. Rainfall accumulations over an inch were common along and east of a line from Joplin to Osage Beach, with isolated accumulations over two inches near Aldrich and Bolivar in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small and large trees were reported down in the Winona area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several shortwaves moved through the region with several waves of showers and storms through the evening on August 25th and into the early morning hours on August 26th. Up to 6 inches of rain fell, along and just south of I-70 over portions of east central Missouri. There were numerous reports of flash flooding in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding across a large portion of Warren County. Numerous roads were flooded including Highway 47, especially north of Warrenton.  MODOT closed both Route TT between Highway 94 and Route T and Route D in Marthasville due to flash flooding. At least one swift water rescue was performed near Marthasville due to the flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Several roads were flooded and closed over the southwestern sections of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed ahead of a weak cold front and an upper level disturbance as they moved southeast across the mid and upper Mississippi Valley. Isolated severe thunderstorms developed by late in the afternoon. During the hottest and most unstable time of day, a few of the storms produced high winds and hail. The storms occurred in a moderate to strongly unstable atmosphere, with mixed layer capes of 1500 to 3000. Effective wind shear values ranged from 30 to 40 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf-ball size hail was reported along Highway 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms sagged southward across southeast Missouri during the afternoon hours. Strong winds along the leading edge of this line produced isolated damage. The line of storms was initially well-organized as it moved into southeast Missouri. Weak low to mid level wind shear was the main limiting factor for a more widespread severe wind event. Strong instability during the hot and humid afternoon hours resulted in the regeneration of intense storm clusters along the line. Although the line of storms became somewhat disorganized as it continued southward, low-level lapse rates were sufficient to support locally damaging winds with the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tall tree was down on a home. A non-shingle protective layer of roofing was blown off an inn along the Current River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm complex that developed over the Foothills of the Rockies in eastern Wyoming on the afternoon of the 25th charged southeastward into northwest Missouri during the  morning of the 26th. A very unstable airmass over the Ozarks invigorated the storm complex as it approached southwest Missouri during the early afternoon. The storms produced widespread winds over 60 mph over western and central Missouri through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on a roof causing minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th and into the early morning hours of May 24th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. On the anniversary of the Joplin Tornado off 2011, another tornado, rated EF-3 struck the north side of Joplin. This storm system eventually produced subsequent tornadoes in Golden City, Eldon and Jefferson City. Unfortunately there were three fatalities as a result of the tornado in Golden City.  The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of high water and flash flooding were reported in and around the Monett area per social media reports. Multiple roads were impassable as Cleek and Kelly Creeks were flowing out of their banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several shortwaves moved through the region with several waves of showers and storms through the evening on August 25th and into the early morning hours on August 26th. Up to 6 inches of rain fell, along and just south of I-70 over portions of east central Missouri. There were numerous reports of flash flooding in this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to six inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. The hardest hit areas were in Eureka, Peerless Park, Fenton, Valley Park, Wildwood and Chesterfield. Near Eureka, a woman drove into flood waters on a rural road not realizing how deep it was. She became stranded and had no cell service. Fortunately her car had OnStar and was able to call for help. She abandoned her vehicle and clung to a tree for 40 minutes before being rescued. Another water rescue was performed at Meramec Station and Smizer Mill Road.  Another driver was trying to turn around on Hwy 109 near LaSalle Springs Middle School to avoid the flood waters when another vehicle coming towards them caused a wave that swapped their car and caused it to stall. A man in a large truck was able to rescue the driver before it floated away.  Highway 141 was flooded once again where it goes under I-44 and had to be closed for a time. Fox Creek just southwest of Allenton rose well above its banks in the southern portion of a large campground. Staff were able to warn people camped in that area to move to higher ground.  The ball fields at Eureka High School were flooded for a second time in a month due to the nearby creek rising well out of its banks. In Wildwood, a horse was swept away by the flood waters. Fortunately he was found safe and sound about a quarter mile downstream. Portions of downtown Eureka were flooded once again. Several businesses sustained flood damage.  No deaths or injuries were reported from the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of funnel clouds were observed in association with showers along a weak cold front. The funnel clouds were of the landspout variety and were not associated with thunderstorm updraft rotation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel clouds were observed in two parts of Perry County, northwest and northeast of Perryville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite another wet month, the Mississippi River dropped below flood stage in August. Drier weather tried to settle into the area, but abundant moisture triggered locally heavy downpours. Rainfall was not widespread enough to generate renewed flooding on the Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The prolonged Mississippi River flooding that began in mid March finally ended in early August. There was still some minor flooding of low-lying fields in early August, otherwise the flooding impacts ended by early August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized complex of thunderstorms moved southeast across southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. The storms over the Ozark foothills, on the southwest edge of the complex, were locally severe. The storms were related to a series of upper level disturbances rotating through the southern periphery of a 500 mb trough from the central Plains to the mid Mississippi Valley. Owing to strong daytime heating and tropical dew points in the mid 70's, the atmosphere was strongly unstable. Mixed layer capes ranged from 2000 to 3000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm survey determined that an intermittent tornado touched down in southeast Springfield, just east of Highway 65 and south of Battlefield Road. The tornado then tracked to the northeast where it crossed Battlefield Road and south Blackman Road. The tornado snapped and broke large tree limbs, bent a few street signs and damaged an aluminum fence. The intermittent path then continued to the northeast where it caused minor roof damage to a home south of Sunset Street. This was the end point of the intermittent tornado path. The maximum winds were estimated at 70mph, with a width of 20 yards and a total distance traveled of 1.5 miles. The tornado started at 1:11 am and ended at 1:15am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV slowly tracked eastward through the forecast area just ahead of a cold front. Numerous showers and storms developed. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of large hail fell from the Sullivan area northeastward towards Stanton. The large hail stone was 2.5 inches in diameter on the north side of Sullivan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV slowly tracked eastward through the forecast area just ahead of a cold front. Numerous showers and storms developed. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage occurred throughout Sullivan, MO. Numerous trees were either snapped off or uprooted. Also, a large portion of the roof of a skating rink was blown off. The section of roof landed on an unoccupied truck in the parking lot. Tree limbs and several power lines were blown down around town. Some of the fallen trees and tree limbs caused minor to moderate damage to nearby homes. County Road 185 near Sullivan was closed for about two hours to remove power lines and snapped poles from the roadway.  No injuries were reported.

NWS Storm Damage Surveys concluded that eight tornadoes occurred along the squall line, affecting locations in and near the communities of Sparta, Rogersville, Highlandville, Licking and Willow Springs, as well as areas on the southeast side of Springfield, and along Wilson���s Creek south of Battlefield. Rainfall accumulations over an inch were common along and east of a line from Joplin to Osage Beach, with isolated accumulations over two inches near Aldrich and Bolivar in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 60mph winds was received via MPing.

An MCV slowly tracked eastward through the forecast area just ahead of a cold front. Numerous showers and storms developed. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches fell in a short amount of time onto already saturated soils, this caused flash flooding in a number of locations. At least a foot and a half of water was flowing over 5th Street in Eureka and the athletic fields at Eureka High School were flooded once again. At the intersection of Chesterfield Parkway and Justus Post Road in Chesterfield, two feet of water was over the roadway and several water rescues had to be performed in the area. Highway 141 was flooded where it goes under I-44 in Valley Park. Another water rescue was performed near the intersection of Meramec Station and Smizer Mill Road in Fenton. A swift water rescue was performed on Fox Creek Crossing Drive where it crosses Little Fox Creek. It was well out of it's banks and a red truck was stranded in the rapidly rising water there. The fire department had to throw a rope and life jacket to the man and pull him to safety.  There was also flowing water over the intersection of Big Bend Road and Sanders Drive and on Manchester Road just east of intersection with Brentwood Blvd. No injuries were reported with any of the rescues.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to four inches of rain in a short amount of time onto already saturated soils caused flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded including the intersection of Highway W and Winnifred Drive.

A storm complex that developed over the Foothills of the Rockies in eastern Wyoming on the afternoon of the 25th charged southeastward into northwest Missouri during the  morning of the 26th. A very unstable airmass over the Ozarks invigorated the storm complex as it approached southwest Missouri during the early afternoon. The storms produced widespread winds over 60 mph over western and central Missouri through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures received via social media of multiple trees uprooted in Anderson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th and into the early morning hours of May 24th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. On the anniversary of the Joplin Tornado off 2011, another tornado, rated EF-3 struck the north side of Joplin. This storm system eventually produced subsequent tornadoes in Golden City, Eldon and Jefferson City. Unfortunately there were three fatalities as a result of the tornado in Golden City.  The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were down on Highway CC 13 miles from West Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rainfall accumulation of 3.90 inches was reported by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low in the mid levels of the atmosphere raced out of the central Rockies and became even stronger as it moved into the upper Midwest. As a result, the surface pressure gradient increased across the region. A cold front swept rapidly eastward during the early morning hours, causing winds to shift into the west and increase even further. Peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 50 mph at most locations. The highest measured gust was 49 mph at a mesonet site on the Mississippi River near New Madrid. There were unofficial gusts near 50 mph in the Ozark foothills, where some tree damage was reported in Carter County.
Storms developed across the region north of a warm front. Some of the storms produced large hail.
Storms developed along an outflow boundary from a decaying MCS. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees at the intersection of Highway OO and Highway F just southeast of Farmington, blocking the roadway for a time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down a tree on the east side of Cuba.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and deep upper level trough affected the region from the early morning of the 10th through the mid-morning hours of the 11th. While some sporadic hail and wind reports were received, this system was much more of a heavy rainfall producer. By the late morning of the 10th, flash flooding was already occurring near the Oklahoma and Kansas borders. A State Highway was closed due to flooding in Ozark County and numerous low water crossings and county roads were flooded in southern and central Missouri. ||The largest amounts of rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 10th, with widespread rainfall amounts of two to three inches east of a Joplin to Osceola line, and isolated amounts over 5 inches near Branson and Eldon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missouri Route 181 was closed in both directions just south of County Road 176 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms moved across the Ozarks. An isolated severe storm produced quarter sized hail near Montier, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters and winds up to 50 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the region from the late evening of the 20th to the pre-dawn hours of the 21st as a cold front and negatively-tilted upper trough moved across the region. Widespread wind damage occurred across southwest and south central Missouri, mainly south of Interstate 44. NWS Storm Damage Surveys concluded that eight tornadoes occurred along the squall line, affecting locations in and near the communities of Sparta, Rogersville, Highlandville, Licking and Willow Springs, as well as areas on the southeast side of Springfield, and along Wilson���s Creek south of Battlefield. Rainfall accumulations over an inch were common along and east of a line from Joplin to Osage Beach, with isolated accumulations over two inches near Aldrich and Bolivar in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th and into the early morning hours of May 24th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. On the anniversary of the Joplin Tornado off 2011, another tornado, rated EF-3 struck the north side of Joplin. This storm system eventually produced subsequent tornadoes in Golden City, Eldon and Jefferson City. Unfortunately there were three fatalities as a result of the tornado in Golden City.  The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway C was closed due to flooding from Little Lindley Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms sagged southward across southeast Missouri during the afternoon hours. Strong winds along the leading edge of this line produced isolated damage. The line of storms was initially well-organized as it moved into southeast Missouri. Weak low to mid level wind shear was the main limiting factor for a more widespread severe wind event. Strong instability during the hot and humid afternoon hours resulted in the regeneration of intense storm clusters along the line. Although the line of storms became somewhat disorganized as it continued southward, low-level lapse rates were sufficient to support locally damaging winds with the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near County Road 630.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th and into the early morning hours of May 24th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. On the anniversary of the Joplin Tornado off 2011, another tornado, rated EF-3 struck the north side of Joplin. This storm system eventually produced subsequent tornadoes in Golden City, Eldon and Jefferson City. Unfortunately there were three fatalities as a result of the tornado in Golden City.  The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue was reported at Highway D and Highway C near Polk where the highway crosses Panther Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms produced heavy rainfall and flooding as a slow-moving cold front moved across southeastern Kansas and into Missouri from overnight April 30th and continued into May 1st. Rainfall accumulations of three to six inches on already very saturated soils led to widespread flooding of low water crossings and larger river systems. In one case, two kayakers perished on Bull Creek when they attempted to float the river after it had risen nearly 14 feet from the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A co-op observer reported a 24-hr rainfall accumulation of 2.03 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey confirmed an EF-1 tornado began along Bryant Creek in southern Douglas County and proceeded towards the northeast throughout the tornado duration. The tornado then crossed Highway 14 and moved to the southeast of Drury and Vanzant before lifting near Highway 76 to the east of Vanzant. Hundreds to thousands of trees were snapped or destroyed along the tornados path, as well as damage to several outbuildings with one outbuilding being completely destroyed. The tornado was on the ground for a total length of 16.45 miles lasting for 15 minutes and had a maximum wind of 100 miles per hour and a maximum path width of a 450 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few small clusters of thunderstorms developed during the heat of the afternoon and tracked slowly southeast. Large hail occurred with the initial storms, then isolated wind damage occurred prior to the weakening of the storms in the evening. The storms formed in a region of strong instability, where mixed layer capes were locally around 4000. A 700 mb high was centered over the Ozark Mountains. Very low wind shear in the vicinity of this high limited the potential for organized severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was mixed in with a lot of pea to half-inch size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms from the previous day left an outflow boundary along the Arkansas border that helped produce strong to severe storms during the afternoon of the 21st. Reports of hail to the size of quarters, wind damage to trees and power lines were received from Nixa to Rolla with the first round of storms. Isolated reports of flash flooding were received with the second and third round of storms from Howell and Newton Counties, where two to three inches of rain fell in a one to two hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike downed a tree on power lines near the Nature Center in Bennett Springs State Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey confirmed an EF-1 tornado started near Bryant Creek to the southwest of Dora in northern Ozark County before crossing into southeastern Douglas County finally lifting northeast of Dora. Two mobile homes were also damaged along Highway CC, with one of the mobile homes losing a roof and being pulled off its foundation. The tornado then crossed into Douglas County just north of County Road 382 just east of Highway 181 and continued for another 7 miles. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized complex of thunderstorms moved southeast across southeast Missouri during the late afternoon hours. The storms over the Ozark foothills, on the southwest edge of the complex, were locally severe. The storms were related to a series of upper level disturbances rotating through the southern periphery of a 500 mb trough from the central Plains to the mid Mississippi Valley. Owing to strong daytime heating and tropical dew points in the mid 70's, the atmosphere was strongly unstable. Mixed layer capes ranged from 2000 to 3000 j/kg.

A storm complex that developed over the Foothills of the Rockies in eastern Wyoming on the afternoon of the 25th charged southeastward into northwest Missouri during the  morning of the 26th. A very unstable airmass over the Ozarks invigorated the storm complex as it approached southwest Missouri during the early afternoon. The storms produced widespread winds over 60 mph over western and central Missouri through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail and small limbs down were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and deep upper level trough affected the region from the early morning of the 10th through the mid-morning hours of the 11th. While some sporadic hail and wind reports were received, this system was much more of a heavy rainfall producer. By the late morning of the 10th, flash flooding was already occurring near the Oklahoma and Kansas borders. A State Highway was closed due to flooding in Ozark County and numerous low water crossings and county roads were flooded in southern and central Missouri. ||The largest amounts of rain occurred during the afternoon and evening of the 10th, with widespread rainfall amounts of two to three inches east of a Joplin to Osceola line, and isolated amounts over 5 inches near Branson and Eldon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency Manager in Kimberling City reported a 24 hour rainfall total of 4.28 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms producing heavy rainfall, severe winds, hail and tornadoes moved across the Missouri Ozarks from May 20th and into the early morning hours of May 24th as upper level disturbances interacted with a slow moving but powerful storm system over the central Plains. Tornadoes occurred with each round, with the strongest and most numerous tornadoes occurring during the late afternoon and evening of the 22nd. On the anniversary of the Joplin Tornado off 2011, another tornado, rated EF-3 struck the north side of Joplin. This storm system eventually produced subsequent tornadoes in Golden City, Eldon and Jefferson City. Unfortunately there were three fatalities as a result of the tornado in Golden City.  The cumulative rainfall caused areas of flash flooding with some flooding lingering across the region. Some locations in southeastern Kansas and southwestern Missouri reported in excess of 8 inches of rainfall over the 72 hour period. .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer measured a 24 hour rainfall total of 3.30 inches.
